title: Drop Strategies
concept: Sometimes it is safer to drop (delete) rows or columns with missing data rather than trying to impute and guess the values.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the main goal of data imputation?

options.text: To fill in missing values with estimated numbers or classes
To compress files so they take up less space

exercises.explain: Right! Imputation fills in empty spots with estimated values like the mean or median to keep data complete.

cards.title: To fill or to destroy?
cards.body: What if a column is 90% blank? Guessing 90% of your data will create a fake dataset. Sometimes, deleting is the only honest choice.

cards.title: Drop Strategies
cards.body: Drop strategies involve removing data from your dataset. You can drop specific rows (records) or entire columns (features) that have missing values.

cards.title: Grading a ruined test
cards.body: If a student skips one question, you grade the rest (imputation/drop row). If the ink spilled and erased the entire back page, you throw that page out (drop column).

cards.title: When to drop a column
cards.body: You're predicting car prices. The "sunroof color" column is 95% blank. Since most cars don't even have a sunroof, it's safest to drop the column entirely.

cards.title: Dropping has hidden costs
cards.body: If you drop every row with even one blank, you might lose 50% of your total rows! This shrinks your dataset, leaving the model with too little to learn from.

cards.title: Drop Row vs Drop Column
cards.body: How to choose your deletion strategy:

left.text:
Delete incomplete rows
Good when few rows have blanks
Reduces your total dataset size

right.text:
Delete incomplete features
Good when column is mostly blank
Removes that feature entirely

exercises.prompt: When is it most appropriate to drop an entire column of data?

options.text: When the column is mostly missing values and lacks useful information
When the column contains numbers instead of text
When you want to speed up your internet connection
When the column has zero missing values

exercises.explain: Exactly! If a column is mostly empty, guessing the missing values is unreliable. Better to drop the whole column.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Dropping rows is always better than dropping columns, no matter how many blanks there are.
exercises.explain: Correct! If you drop rows for every blank, you might accidentally delete a large portion of your dataset.

exercises.sentence: If a column is 99% blank, the safest strategy is to drop the ___.
exercises.explain: Yes! With 99% missing, there's not enough real information to keep the column.

exercises.prompt: Which one is an example of dropping a row?

options.text: Deleting the entire record of a customer who left their income blank
Deleting the 'income' column for all customers
Replacing a missing income value with the average income

exercises.explain: Correct! Dropping a row removes that specific record (horizontal slice) while leaving the rest of the columns intact.

exercises.prompt: You are training a model to predict student grades. 2% of students forgot to write their middle names, and 80% forgot to list their phone numbers. What is the best strategy?

options.text: Drop the phone number column, and drop the rows for the 2% missing middle names (or impute them)
Drop the middle name column, and keep the phone number column
Drop all student rows with any missing value
Impute all phone numbers with a constant default like '555-0000'

exercises.explain: Perfect choice! The phone column is mostly empty, so drop it. The middle name is only missing in 2% of rows, so dropping those rows or imputing is very safe. Now, let's learn how to track what was missing!
